Bródno (9th/10th century): A small fortified settlement (gród) in the north-eastern part of modern Warsaw, destroyed around 1040.
Kamion (11th century): A settlement near the present-day Warszawa Wschodnia station.
Jazdów (12th/13th century): A nearby fortified, residential, and administrative settlement that was destroyed, leading to the development of the new site. Warsaw has existed since approximately 1300, serving primarily as the capital of Poland (or its predecessor states) for most of its history, though it was occupied by Prussia, Russia, and Germany during the partitions (1795–1918) and WWII. It is currently the capital of the Republic of Poland, a role it officially held from 1596–1795 and 1918–present. 
Key Historical Affiliations (Approx. 1300–Present):
c. 1300–1526: Duchy of Masovia (independent/Polish vassal): Founded as a trading post, later becoming the capital of the Duchy of Masovia.
1526–1795: Kingdom of Poland/Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth: Incorporated into Poland (1526), becoming the seat of the Sejm (1569) and the official capital (1596).
1795–1806: Kingdom of Prussia: Annexed by Prussia during the Third Partition of Poland, becoming part of the province of South Prussia.
1807–1815: Duchy of Warsaw: Created by Napoleon Bonaparte as a French puppet state.
1815–1918: Russian Empire (Congress Poland): Following Napoleon’s defeat, it became the capital of a kingdom linked to Russia, eventually losing autonomy.
1918–1939: Republic of Poland (Second Republic): Restored as the capital of independent Poland.
It was a part of Germany in 1939-1945
Then a part of Poland for the rest of its life

The loneliness here is REAL! And it's funny how many people feel lonely, so you think "Hey, both of you feel lonely, why don't you connect then?" - but it just doesn't happen... Personally, I'd say it's my biggest source of pain here. Making new connections is not easy, especially if you're not in any systems that bring people together, like universities (work doesn't count as it facilitates business relationships for the most part, not casual connections). So I'm trying to make friends by "putting myself" in such systems, like the vogueing scene, 12-step meetings, yoga classes, cinemas - basically anything that increases chances of meeting like-minded people (I speak Polish, but there are circles where people communicate in English too). I'm so desperate that even sharing a gaze with a stranger on public transportation can make my day lol

To be honest, I find Warsaw quite boring for a capital city. The service culture can feel very rigid, even compared to other European cities, and some people get upset when you speak English. Many museums are also difficult to fully enjoy if you don’t speak Polish. Poles are also very unfriendly even when compared to other Europeans. Of course not everyone is the same, I have one Polish friend and she is great.

That said, it’s very safe. The only crimes I’ve really heard about involve locals having their cars stolen.

But I’ve also encountered racism and homophobia that reminded me more of rednecks from the USA than of a capital in the EU.

The city lacks charm and the people tend to be not friendly and borderline rude at times but very proud.

Warsaw isn’t just another big city in Poland — it’s a place wrapped in a kind of myth, both admired and critisized. For many people outside the capital, the city feels like it’s all about chasing success: ego, ambition, sometimes more about showing off than being real. That sense of loneliness you hear about from people living there? It comes from being surrounded by strangers — folks who came looking for opportunity, bold, direct, sometimes a bit rough. And yeah, most of today’s “Warszawiaks” aren’t old families with deep roots, but newcomers from the east, the south, small towns, who wanted a faster, bigger life.

Psychology gives a way to see this. In typologies inspired by Jung, you hear about “red” personalities: dominant, result-driven, self-confident, pushy. Warsaw attracts and rewards exactly that kind of energy. But when that style sets the tone for the whole city, there’s less space for empathy, patience, community — traits you’d link with “green” or “blue” types, more calm, thoughtful, relational.

That’s where the negative PR comes from. To many outside, Warsaw looks shallow, full of people who made money but didn’t really grow as persons, who put on a show of success without depth. The city feels like a stage for the rat race, dominated by ambitious individuals fighting for their own. Smaller towns don’t create that same pressure, so the contrast is sharp, sometimes even shocking.

It doesn’t mean Warsaw is “bad”. It’s the capital, the center of business, tech, politics, culture, education. It gives energy, options, variety. But when people focus on what they think it lacks — roots, community, humility — the criticism grows. It’s not only envy, it’s about values. Between those who chase ambition and individual success, and those who want stability, connection, simplicity. Warsaw stands strong for the first, and many just wish there was more of the second.

From my personal experience, the further north you go in Europe, the harder it gets to build genuine human connections. Poland is already leaning that way, close to Scandinavia, even the weather feels almost the same. Meanwhile, in South-Eastern Europe and the Balkans, friendships form much more naturally. Of course, you won’t always get the same level of order or infrastructure you see in Poland or Scandinavia, but you will find warmer vibes, better energy, and sunnier skies.

I honestly believe weather shapes people more than we realize. In Central and Northern Europe, you’ve got long, dark winters and endless rain,  people stay inside, become more withdrawn and over time even a bit alienated or depressed. They also become "work alcoholics" as they mistakenly believe that money will fix the original problem. If you feel depressed no matter where you are, that’s an inner issue you need to face. But if it only happens in certain countries, then maybe it’s not you, maybe it’s the vibe of that place. And i will take it even further and say it can actually be a sign you shouldn't be there to begin with, that you are not meant to be there. Not every land resonates with everyone’s mentality or energy.

Don’t count on meeting people just by walking around the streets or sitting in bars drinking beer, waiting for someone to approach you or for you to approach someone. You need to join a group that’s connected with some activity — sign up for a course, ideally a Polish language course. There you’ll meet other foreigners who face the exact same challenges as you do with adapting to life in Warsaw. This way you’ll make your first friends, and through them you’ll meet more people, and things will start to grow from there.

Sign up for a dance class or any kind of activity. When I first came to Warsaw, even though I’m Polish, I also felt lonely at the beginning. But I had to take action, get involved in something, and soon enough I had quite a few friends. Sitting around passively won’t bring any results. As always, you need conscious and intentional effort to reach a concrete outcome — whether in this area of life or in any other

My husband and I watched your video about Vilnius, Lithuania, tonight. 
We have never responded to anything online. However, it was so important that we figured a way to do so.
To provide context, my husband was born and raised in Vilnius from 1962 to ‘75. These years, are of course before your time; but, we’re reaching out to tell you that your video resonated so much with him. He lived under the Russian regime and fled to North America in 1975. You weren’t born then, but your video captured the”storey book” essence of his home town. It resonated with us so much that we had to reach out. Your video brought my husband to tears.

THINGS YOU CAN DO FOR FREE IN BELGRADE >>> As of 1/1/2025, public transportation is free (except the A1 shuttle bus from the airport and "E" mini buses that you may see here and there). Buses, trams, trolley-buses, and electric buses are free, like a hop on - hop off system. Bus 72 from the airport to Zeleni Venac (wider city center) is also free - with just a short walk to the main pedestrian street which is in the center. Entrance to the Belgrade Fortress and the Saint Sava Temple is free. Entrance to all religious objects is free. Entrance to all parks is free (except the Botanical Garden). Some museums offer free admission on certain days. For example, the National Museum is free on Sundays, others may be giving free admission on other days, you have to Google it, depending on your interests (Belgrade has 52 registered museums, small and large). Entrance to the Ada Lake/beach is free. So, quite a bit of free stuff to do. You can always sit on a park bench and do people-watching, enjoy the sunset at the fortress or at the very impressive Monument to the Unknown Hero at Avala Mountain, walk and ride as much as you want... Tap water is safe and potable, so you don't have to keep on buying bottled water. You can refill at your apartment or hotel, or at that public fountain in the main pedestrian street. If you eat at bakeries and fast food places, you can get a meal for a few bucks. So, done right, Belgrade can be quite affordable.
You don't need to tip in Serbia. You just round up. For example your bill is 470 dinars, you round to 500. Thats like 2-3 cents.

Bus passes are tricky to get hold of - you won't be able to get them from the kiosks dotted around the city as they say in the guide books. The only place you can get them is at 6, Kneza Mihaila, next to the Belgrade Cultural Centre. Go through the ordinary glass front door, then up the stairs to the second floor. We went there a few weeks ago and they didn't have any 3-day passes, only 1-day (which you can't buy in advance, they have to be used on that day), 7-day or 30-day. They 7-day pass costs 800 dinars.
